speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape five with Kaethe Wells.

speaker: interviewer
text: Miss Wells, what was on that postcard?

speaker: interviewee
text: The son wrote to his mother, 'I have been bad, and I have stolen. And so I am now here in prison. Go to gospozha Schohl-- her husband, [GERMAN] Dr. Schohl, is here with me in prison. And he said to tell his family that he is well, and he sends his love.' I mean, it was black on white, we couldn't understand. And then we got the map out-- and we said, Zagreb, Tyrolia, it's on the way to the east-- it had to be. We never-- that was the last we ever heard from our father. That was the last we actually knew he was there. Everything else that comes is guesswork.

speaker: interviewer
text: The following year, August 1944, a gestapo sergeant came and said, Mother, come. When Mother came back, he was with her. He said, children, our father is dead. He died in concentration camp Auschwitz, December 9th of last year. What she told us was, when she went in there, and the gestapo told her that you are being sent back to Germany. And my mother said to join my husband? Surely my husband will be working for Germany. Oh, didn't anybody tell you? No. He took a little piece of paper out of the drawer and said, Max Schohl, Jew number so-and-so, died of a heart attack, December 9, 1943, concentration camp Auschwitz. So we never knew-- who are we going to believe? Oh, my mother said, may I have this? No. And he put it back.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had nothing. We had trouble after the war to prove that our father was dead. To this day, we have nothing to prove he is dead. We tried and tried and tried. Well, we never found out. We tend to believe the general-- that it makes us feel better to think that he never entered the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: While your father was gone, did you have enough food to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had Serbian friends, and the lady who lived with us had a big garden. And we lived mostly on fruits and vegetables from the garden.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were still there, did you have any further contact with any of the gypsies?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We never saw them again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see them being persecuted--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or hear of them being persecuted?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever witness any additional hangings or shootings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, shootings almost every day. We did not go back to the marketplace.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see anyone who survived who had arrived there with you when you first arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did not see them but through the grapevine, we heard that there was another family alive, beside us. Their Serbian landlord had a root cellar, and in 1941, when they rounded up all the Jews of Ruma, it was-- Lily Karolyi was a famous actress on the Burgtheater in Vienna. Her brother was a journalist, and he was married to a German woman-- not Jewish. That Serbian friend, landlord, made them a little room in the root cellar and fed them. And at night, they came up and got air and walked around. And they survived all these years, but we never saw them. But we understood that his mind was not so good. But they were alive and went back to Vienna. That's what we heard.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long after your father died did you stay there where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ruma?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Ruma.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what were the circumstances surrounding your leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were arrested and sent back to Germany. We didn't ask why.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came to arrest you?

speaker: interviewee
text: A gestapo sergeant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me that day what happened to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came and took my mother across the street to the gestapo. And he came back with my mother, and she said, pack only what you can carry-- pack warm things. We're going back to Germany. We didn't dare open our mouth. He was there, watched us pack, and then he took the keys.

speaker: interviewer
text: And unfortunately, the lady was not there. But the four of us-- he walked behind us. The three of us walked to the station. And on the way to the station, you know, people waved to us-- going on a trip? Mm-hm, going on a trip.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, after we were gone, everybody knew what happened. We sat-- it was so hot. It was such a hot day. And we didn't dare ask him anything. Surely everybody who saw us must have seen that we were arrested. We sat on our suitcases at the station. And he stood in the shade and we waited. All of a sudden, a cattle car came. We were like, oh, my God. And I don't know if you've ever seen pictures of cattle cars with these tiny windows, and those arms sticking out. And the train stopped. And my sister said, they're not going to get me on the train. I'm going to kill myself before then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we looked, and he just stood there. And my mother said, let's just-- don't do anything, let's just wait. But right in front of us-- they must have come from Italy, because signora, signora-- they wanted water. And finally my mother couldn't stand it anymore, and she turned around and she said to him, may I bring them water? No! I said, Mother, please, just-- just sit. All of a sudden, the train-- you know, human beings are such selfish animals. As the train started to roll, oh, thank God. Such a sigh of relief, such selfishness that we're not on this train. Isn't that horrible? But that's the way we are. Well, they didn't put us on the cattle train. Now what? I have no idea how long we sat there-- all of a sudden, a regular train came full of soldiers. And all the soldiers had the uniform on from the Africa Corps. They came from Africa-- Rommel had lost. They were all going, heim ins reich, back home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came and said, follow me. Followed him, and he was looking and looking, and one of the cars had a little sign that said [GERMAN] one warden, three prisoners. And he said, up. We went up, and the soldiers went, ah, Fraulein, Fraulein. And we looked at him, and he said [POINTING] And we walked in there, and that compartment was full of soldiers. But on one side-- there were wooden benches-- on one side there was that sign again, one warden, three prisoners. And we sat down. We knew that was us. And across from us were all these German soldiers. And when we sat down, they burst out laughing, and laughed, and laughed, and said, leave it to our German women. They will think of anything to get back home. They thought we dreamt this-- because you see, all the Germans, or the German generation of Germans, knew what was happening, and they wanted to get back. You couldn't. The trains were soldiers-- that's all. And they knew that. And so they thought, our German women, how smart they are. And this is their friend, and that's how they'll get. And they talked to us like we were, you know-- the gestapo guy did not open his mouth.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were out of Germany, before you got back on the train, did you ever have news of the war? Did you-- did you know what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there rumors that you heard about what was going on back in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The only rumors we heard in Ruma, from, through the grapevine, is that the British are coming from the south, and the Russians are coming from here, and Germany has lost the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was all of the money gone that your mother had hidden in the leather pouch?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, jewelry she had.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- well the-- The money from the jewelry, or-- or the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure, we lived on that for a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now when you were going where you didn't know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given food or water?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German soldiers shared their food and their water with us. And the guy didn't open his mouth.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you on that train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I know this was daytime, and I know we went through the night-- probably late the following afternoon. When we went-- one of the soldiers said, we are now in Budapest. The train stopped. And a lot of them went out, and the Red Cross was at the station-- German Red Cross, naturally-- was at the station. And they got on the train again. And everybody, of course, was smoking in those days. They brought us cigarettes. And then we went through the countryside, and there was air raid and everybody out. And my mother would say-- we were-- we were in the ditches with the rest of them. The gestapo guy was right there laying with us, you know, in the ditches. And we kept hoping they would bomb the train and see what happens then but they didn't. We had to get back on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived at your destination, what was the very first thing that you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said to us-- no, my mother said to him, where are we? And he said, in Vienna. And she said, what are we going to do here? He said, I have to take you to gestapo headquarters in Vienna, and then I'll have to say goodbye to you. He was not one of the worst kind. At least he left us alone. We went-- it's unbelievable-- we went on a trolley, on a street car. He knew we couldn't run away. We had no papers. We had nothing. So he wasn't worried about us running away. The four of us got on and got off and, oh, what a gorgeous palace-- that was the prison. Went in there, and I cannot tell you how-- our cell-- oh, that's-- our cell [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] must have been once a gorgeous room. But all the windows had been cemented over. It was round. It was like a cage, actually. They made a, you know, a cage on one side, all the others walls, and three hard chairs in the middle. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They took one by one. We were never questioned together. They took Mother first, and then they took one by one. Day and night, the same questions. Mother would put her lips to our ear and say, always say the same thing. They want to know our contact with the partisans. We know nothing. And it was very difficult, you know, day after day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they torture you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The only thing is, we were so thirsty-- we weren't hungry-- we begged for water. We never got water. And that makes you light-headed, and you sometimes don't know what you are saying. And you sit. My mother was a really-- showed us how to do it. You can fall asleep sitting up straight and that's what we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many other prisoners were there, if you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with any of the other prisoners there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. I try to remember-- it couldn't have been more than days, maybe a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the sleeping conditions like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no sleeping. We sat on the chairs and taught ourselves to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of food were you given?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed to stay with your mother and sister at all times?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three of us, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of toilet facilities did they have?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I don't remember that. I have been trying to think about-- I know we had to go sometimes, but I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: This commandant called us one day together for the first time, the three of us, and he said, here are three pieces of paper that will get you on the train to Wiesbaden. When you are in Wiesbaden, you will report to the Paulinenstrasse, which was the infamous gestapo street. And go to the gestapo, and they will tell you where to go from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was when we were-- that was when we were told we were to be separated. My mother worked there, and we were to work-- it was a gestapo factory. It was called [GERMAN]. And we had a room together with a warden-- a gestapo woman with a gold swastika. She was very tall, black hair and-- [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What, if you know, was the significance of the gold swastika?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we saw somebody who had a gold swastika on, our heart fell in our shoes. We were that afraid, because when Hitler first started, '22, 1923, he had maybe 100-- I don't know, it could be 500. I don't know how many-- joined with him. And when he came to power in '33, to thanks and in appreciation to his old cronies, he had these special swastikas made. They had the gold band. She wore it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: We never talked, with the exception of-- no, we-- we walked to work every morning, but she didn't even say anything like that. I don't think we ever talked until the last day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she cruel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She just watched us. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your living conditions at that time, when you were working at the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had one room. There was a toilet in that room. There was one bed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the windows were, of course, boarded up. There was no glass anyplace. Our suitcases were there. Oh, and a tin bowl and a bucket with water.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many hours a day did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: We left when it was still dark, and that was-- well, it was August, September. And so it was early. And we came home when it was dark, because it was fairly far to walk. Mother usually was there when-- when we came.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were trained as mechanics. There were huge machines. Some were printing machines, some were cutting machines.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they turn out at that factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: The printing machines-- I was never on a printing machine, so I really don't know what was printed. But the cutting machines were shoe soles-- insoles for the shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you work there? How long were you there from the time you arrived, until liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Liberation was in April. We got there in August of '44, and we were liberated April 26th, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's health like then?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was strong. She was always strong, although we knew she had a bad heart. And she has, at that time, already had several little strokes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there any facilities for medication?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know or did you know the whereabouts of any of the other people who worked at the factory? Were they housed near you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We weren't allowed to talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Half Jews in one room. That's where the young ones were. And the other room were women and men. And from what I learned, the Jewish part of the mixed marriage was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were people punished?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've never seen it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they rewarded?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I've never seen it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you transported back and forth to work daily?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it matter if it was raining or cold?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sanitary conditions, were they acceptable at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, they were indoor. The only thing is, you had to use a bucket of water. Nothing worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kept you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our will to survive. We must survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you cry? Did your Mother cry?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Our Father said to us one time, never let them see you are afraid. Never cry in front of them-- cry on the inside, never cry in front of them, and always hold your head up high.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of all the things that you witnessed, what, if you can pick just one, was among the most painful of your memories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Kristallnacht was one, and the last time I saw my Father-- I knew it would have been the last time. That was, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was, would you say, perhaps the most outstanding act of kindness that you witnessed during all of this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were [SIGHS] several. The most astounding were the German soldiers, who, at one time brought us a goose when they saw what we had to eat. Our Serbian friends, of course. The Serbians used to say the Jews and the Serbians are brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any infamous people?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war-- I mean, after our liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, then, about who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 200-- now, I'm probably going to say that wrong. They had a little axe on their sleeve, and they were called the Trail Blazers. And I believe it was the 274th Infantry Regiment, but I could be mistaken, their number, here or there. But they were the Trail Blazers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that. Tell me about the-- the moment you knew that you were liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was tragic, really. That was one of our sadder moments, our liberation. We were sitting in the basement-- that thing was one of the very few times she-- she-- let us go in the basement. And she sat next to us on the bench.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of a sudden, a little boy came downstairs and cried the Americans are here. Looked at her. She sat like a statue. We didn't dare breathing. I mean, what are we going to do? And all of a sudden, she got up, never looked at us, and started to walk up the steps. And I don't know why, but I saw something glittering-- it was the swastika. Now, see this is a dumb thing. I should have saved it. I picked it up and it did me so good when I said, here, Frau so and so.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she didn't want it. She took it, never said a word. We never saw her again. We took over her room-- she with rooms-- we took those over. And we sat, and finally Mother said, I guess we're going up. We're free. Oh. No emotion, nothing. You know, we used to dream that we would scream and laugh and cry and dance-- nothing. Cold, total coldness on the inside. Went upstairs, and Mother said, well, we may as well start cleaning up. And I said, I want to wash myself. And no, not yet, not yet. But the first time we saw the American, there was a courtyard, and we took water and our dirty clothes and just-- and all of a sudden, stop! Mm mm. There was two American soldiers-- one tall, one not so tall-- filthy, dirty, with a drawn gun.

speaker: interviewer
text: Never seen a more beautiful sight in my life. I couldn't even make out the facial features. But, oh, they were beautiful. And I had made up a little speech. Just stood there and looked at them. And they said, guns, soldiers? They were looking for soldiers. And my mother said, for heaven's sake, tell them who we are. And I said, we're Jews. And they looked at each other and said, another one. Everybody in Germany is Jewish or had a Jewish grandmother. And that made me mad. And when I get mad, oh, boy. And I started to talk, and I said, we are Jews. And the little one said, this is Lieutenant Michael Ruckman from Brooklyn. And he's Jewish. And I said, you are Jewish? What's the holiest prayer, and what does a boy do when-- she's examining me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, they were wonderful. They brought us Jeep-loads of food. And he is the one who-- I said to him one day-- he told us they were on their way to Japan. But he said, when I leave, I will turn you all over to-- I said, I've got to earn money. We don't have anything. He's the one who got me-- who got this Major Walker, I think. No, yeah, Walker, I think was his name. That's how I became a hostess in an enlisted man's club, interpreter between the German waiters and the American company, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: During all of this time, was there one particular fear that overwhelmed you? Was there anything that you were particularly afraid of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, because every day one foot was in the grave. You never knew, are you going to be alive tomorrow morning? I mean, every day was your last day. And that lasted a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was in Charleston. I was married. That's before I had children. And we were riding in the car, and I said to my husband, there's a police car behind us. And he said, don't you ever do that again. So what? I said, pull over. Well, anyway, this is too long a story. The fear was still in me. And my husband, who was friendly with the Charleston police, had the guy come to the store so that I could talk to him. I was shaking. Do you still, today, think of the Shoah often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's part of your life. You dream, you-- you-- you-- you never-- you never forget it-- you never forget it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you hold bitterness?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did for awhile. But I'm so thankful for the life I have today. All I do is thank God.
